name: IT Managed Services
description: Managed service agreements from the first network assessment to a countersigned SLA, with a Contracts object that tracks tier, endpoint count and renewal date.
ai_context: You are a CRM assistant for an IT managed service provider (MSP). Contacts are business owners, IT managers and office managers at client and prospect companies; a company is the account you support and a deal is one managed-services agreement being sold or renewed. Pricing is almost always per endpoint or per user with a monthly recurring value and an SLA tier. Never invent SLA response times, endpoint counts, licence positions or audit findings — if the number is not in the record, ask for it.

automation_rules.name: Triage a new IT enquiry within a day
automation_rules.description: Most MSP enquiries arrive because something just broke. This books the discovery call while the pain is still fresh.

params.title: Discovery call with {{contact.first_name}} — endpoint count, who supports them today, what broke
New IT enquiry logged
params.body: {{contact.first_name}} {{contact.last_name}} came in as a new enquiry. Before quoting anything, capture endpoint and server count, the incumbent provider and when that contract ends, plus any compliance obligation. No proposal goes out before a network assessment.

automation_rules.name: Recap the client after every stage move
automation_rules.description: Managed-services deals stall in silence. Every stage change earns a written recap so the buyer can sell it internally.

params.title: Send {{contact.first_name}} a written recap of {{deal.title}} — what changed, what you need next
Stage change on {{deal.title}}
params.body: Record what moved this deal: assessment findings shared, SLA tier agreed, redlines returned, or a blocker. Note the next decision date and who owns it on the client side.

kb_templates.company:
## About us

We are a managed service provider supporting businesses across [your region]. Replace this with the client size you are built for (for example 10–150 endpoints), the verticals you know best, and how long you have been trading.

## Reaching the helpdesk

Ticket email, helpdesk phone, portal URL, and staffed hours.

## Out of hours

State plainly what counts as an emergency and which tiers get out-of-hours cover.
kb_templates.products:
## Managed service tiers

- **Bronze** — business-hours helpdesk, patch management, EDR/antivirus, monitoring and alerting.
- **Silver** — Bronze plus extended hours, backup monitoring and testing, vendor liaison.
- **Gold / Platinum** — 24/7 response, scheduled on-site visits, quarterly vCIO reviews.

## Project work (quoted separately)

Migrations, network refreshes, Microsoft 365 or Google Workspace tenant work, office moves, security remediation.

## Pricing

List your per-endpoint and per-user rates, minimum seat count, and — just as important — what sits explicitly OUT of scope.
kb_templates.playbook:
## Qualifying an MSP prospect

1. Endpoint and server count. This is what you price on, so get it before anything else.
2. Who supports them today and when that agreement ends. Chasing a client 11 months into a 12-month contract is the whole game.
3. What broke recently. Pain closes managed services; feature lists do not.
4. Compliance obligations — HIPAA, PCI-DSS, CMMC, ISO 27001 — because they set the tier and the price floor.
5. Who signs, and whether the budget is operational or capital.

## The assessment is the sale

Never send a price before a network assessment. Present the findings live, show the risk you found in their own environment, and price the remediation project and the ongoing agreement in the same conversation.

## Handling "we already have someone"

Do not attack the incumbent. Ask what response time they were promised and what they actually get. Let the gap do the work.
kb_templates.process:
## From enquiry to signed agreement

1. **New Enquiry** — log the contact, endpoint count and who supports them now.
2. **Discovery Call** — pain, compliance obligations, incumbent contract end date, signing authority.
3. **Network Assessment** — audit endpoints, backups, patch state, licensing and security posture.
4. **Proposal Sent** — assessment findings first, then per-endpoint pricing and the recommended SLA tier.
5. **Contract & SLA Review** — MSA, response-time targets, and the out-of-scope schedule.
6. **Contract Signed** — countersigned. Create the Contract record with its renewal date, then start onboarding and tooling rollout.

Deals that go quiet at Proposal Sent are usually missing a signer, not a discount.
kb_templates.competitors:
## Why clients leave their last provider

Slow response, no proactive patching, a one-person shop with no holiday cover, and invoices that arrive as a surprise.

## Our answer

Replace with your genuine differentiators — documented SLA response targets you can evidence from ticket data, a named engineer who knows the site, flat per-endpoint billing with no hourly surprises, quarterly vCIO reviews.

Never quote a response time you cannot prove from your own ticketing history.
